definition: *Oneiromancy* is divination by dreams — the interpretation of what is seen in sleep as a message about waking events to come. Where most of the *-mancy* arts read an external medium, oneiromancy reads the diviner's own visions, treating the dream as a coded text to be decoded rather than a sign to be observed.
history: The word joins Greek *oneiros* ('dream') to *manteia* ('divination'). Its founding text is Artemidorus' second-century *Oneirocritica*, the ancient world's great dream-dictionary, whose author, scholars note, presents himself not merely as an oneiromancer but as an expert in textual practices. The art never lost its hold: Kieckhefer records that in the medieval West the interpretation of dreams remained popular, as did treatises suggesting how to interpret them. Popular and learned dream-books circulated side by side, and the Enlightenment's mockers still had to list oneiromancy among the divinations they meant to bury.

def_sources.label: Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages
def_sources.abstract: A widely used introduction to what magic actually was in medieval Europe: who practiced it, what forms it took, and how it connected to religion, science, storytelling, and law. It also explains how fear of magic fed into the witch trials of later centuries.

def_sources.label: Artemidorus, Oneirocritica (via Edmonds et al., Magic and Religion in the Ancient Mediterranean)
def_sources.abstract: Written in the second century CE, this is the oldest surviving guide to interpreting dreams, cataloguing thousands of dream images and explaining what each was believed to mean for the dreamer's real life, treating dream interpretation as a practical skill much like fortune-telling.

commentary: Dream-divination's modern historiography runs through Artemidorus' *Oneirocritica* and the twentieth century's fascination with it. Freud cited him; Michel Foucault made the *Oneirocritica* the centerpiece of *The Care of the Self*, reading it for an ethics of the dreaming subject rather than a key to the future; S. R. F. Price ('The Future of Dreams') recovered it instead as a social practice woven into ancient status and exchange. The debate is less about what dreams 'mean' than about how a second-century dream-book became a mirror in which each era reads its own theory of the self.

commentary_sources.label: S. R. F. Price, 'The Future of Dreams: From Freud to Artemidorus'
commentary_sources.abstract: This article compares how the ancient Greek dream-interpretation manual by Artemidorus explained dreams to how Sigmund Freud later explained them, arguing that ancient dream interpreters were doing something different from modern psychology rather than a cruder version of the same thing.

commentary_sources.label: Michel Foucault, The Care of the Self
commentary_sources.abstract: The third volume of Foucault's History of Sexuality looks at how Greek and Roman writers in the first two centuries CE thought about pleasure, self-discipline, and bodily health. Foucault argues that during this period attitudes shifted toward more caution and anxiety about sex, compared with earlier classical Greek views.

etymology.note: Dream-divination, systematised in Artemidorus' 2nd-c. Oneirocritica — antiquity's dream dictionary.
